agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and deliberately vague, leaving significant implementation details undefined. This is an early-stage product concept communicated informally.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined metric representing customer request volume relative to available capacity — exact measurement method unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes — thresholds unspecified" },
      { "term": "Real-time", "definition": "Pricing adjustments occur continuously or near-continuously in response to demand signals — latency tolerance unspecified" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No human approval required for individual price changes within guardrail bounds" },
      { "term": "Competitive", "definition": "Prices should not significantly exceed market alternatives, implying awareness of competitor pricing or customer price sensitivity" },
      { "term": "Profitable", "definition": "Prices must cover delivery costs and contribute margin — floor price must exceed cost basis" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must adjust in response to demand levels — higher demand produces higher prices, lower demand produces lower prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. The direction of correlation (demand↑ → price↑) is a business strategy choice, not a physical or data-integrity constraint. A VP could decide to run a flat-rate promotion or invert the model for market share capture.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 at the same time and location, the computed price for D1 must be greater than the computed price for D2, all else being equal",
      "confidence": "HIGH — explicitly stated by CEO"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real-time, meaning the system must re-evaluate pricing continuously as demand changes rather than on a batch/scheduled basis",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a performance and freshness policy. The business could decide to batch-update prices every 5 minutes and the system would not be corrupt — just staler.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand changes, the price offered to the next customer must reflect the updated demand level within the system's defined latency threshold",
      "confidence": "HIGH — explicitly stated by CEO"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The pricing system must operate without manual human intervention for individual pricing decisions",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Full automation is a business process choice. The business could add manual approval for high-value orders or override capabilities without corrupting the system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price calculations and adjustments execute end-to-end without requiring human approval steps in the critical path",
      "confidence": "HIGH — explicitly stated ('fully automated')"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A price ceiling (upper guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum threshold",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without an upper bound, the automated system could produce absurd or legally problematic prices (e.g., $10,000 for a local delivery). This is a safety constraint protecting system integrity, brand reputation, and potentially regulatory compliance. An uncapped automated pricing system is a corrupt system.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may exceed the configured maximum price ceiling, regardless of demand level",
      "confidence": "MEDIUM — CEO said 'probably have some guardrails so prices don't go crazy', implying guardrails are intended but not yet firmly committed. The word 'probably' introduces ambiguity."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A price floor (lower guardrail) must exist to ensure prices never fall below the cost of fulfillment",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Pricing below cost violates the stated goal of profitability and represents a data/state integrity issue — the system is producing economically invalid outputs. A delivery priced below its cost is a system error, not a business decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may fall below the cost basis of fulfilling that delivery",
      "confidence": "MEDIUM — implied by 'profitable' goal and 'guardrails' mention, but the CEO did not explicitly state a floor. The profitability requirement logically demands it."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Prices must remain competitive relative to market alternatives",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Competitiveness is a strategic preference, not a hard constraint. The business may choose to be premium-priced in certain segments. A VP could override this for specific markets.",
      "testable_assertion": "UNTESTABLE as stated — no definition of 'competitive' provided. Requires: reference benchmark (competitor prices? historical prices? customer willingness-to-pay?) and acceptable deviation threshold.",
      "confidence": "LOW — 'competitive' is subjective and undefined. No benchmark, no threshold, no measurement method specified."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The system must maintain profitability — aggregate pricing outcomes must exceed aggregate delivery costs",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Profitability is a business goal measured over time, not a per-transaction invariant. The business may accept individual loss-leader deliveries as long as the aggregate is profitable.",
      "testable_assertion": "Over any configured measurement period, total delivery revenue must exceed total delivery cost (margin > 0)",
      "confidence": "HIGH — explicitly stated by CEO, though measurement period and granularity (per-delivery vs aggregate) are unspecified"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Demand must be quantifiable — the system requires a defined, measurable demand signal to drive pricing decisions",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without a computable demand signal, the pricing function has no input and cannot produce a price. This is a precondition for the system to function at all — it's a data integrity requirement.",
      "testable_assertion": "The system must have a current demand metric available before computing any price; if demand signal is unavailable, the system must fall back to a defined default behavior rather than producing an arbitrary price",
      "confidence": "HIGH — logically required by the 'based on demand' mechanism, though the CEO did not specify what demand means (order volume? capacity utilization? request rate?)"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal becomes unavailable (data feed failure, sensor outage)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — must decide: freeze at last known price? Fall back to base rate? Halt quoting? This is critical for a 'fully automated' system." },
    { "scenario": "Demand spike so extreme that even the ceiling price cannot cover costs (e.g., natural disaster causing massive cost increases)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — ceiling may conflict with profitability floor if costs spike above the ceiling" },
    { "scenario": "Demand is zero or near-zero", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — does the floor apply? Could the system price at $0 or offer free delivery as a demand stimulus?" },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request delivery at the same instant but see different prices due to real-time fluctuation", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — fairness and consistency policy not addressed" },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price but by checkout time demand has shifted and price changed", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — price lock/quote validity window not specified. This is a major UX and trust concern." },
    { "scenario": "Competitor drops prices dramatically, making our floor price uncompetitive", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — conflict between 'competitive' and 'profitable' goals with no stated priority" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered during emergencies (e.g., surge pricing during natural disasters)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no mention of regulatory compliance. In many jurisdictions, automated surge pricing during emergencies is illegal." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail thresholds need to change — who can modify them and how quickly?", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — governance model for guardrail configuration not specified" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-25% if well-calibrated, but can destroy demand if perceived as unfair. Margin protection via price floor is critical. Risk of regulatory fines if price-gouging laws are violated.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with sub-second response times, monitoring/alerting for guardrail breaches, and fallback mechanisms. Significant operational complexity increase over static pricing.",
    "user": "High risk to customer trust. Customers generally distrust dynamic pricing (Uber surge pricing backlash is well-documented). Price transparency, quote validity windows, and perceived fairness are critical UX concerns not addressed in the spec.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Automated pricing with vague guardrails is a reputational and regulatory time bomb. Without explicit price-gouging protections, competitive benchmarking, and quote validity rules, this system could produce legally or ethically problematic outcomes at scale with no human in the loop.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Will see variable prices — may perceive unfairness, may lose trust if prices swing visibly. Need transparency on why prices vary." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Not mentioned at all — if driver compensation is tied to delivery price, this directly affects their earnings. Major omission." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Must define profitability thresholds, cost basis calculations, and margin targets that feed the pricing engine" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and pricing transparency requirements by jurisdiction" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability — need policies for price disputes, refund criteria, and explanation scripts" },
      { "group": "Competitors", "impact": "May trigger pricing wars if dynamic pricing undercuts market aggressively during low demand" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The spec is an informal CEO statement at an all-hands meeting — not a product requirements document. It contains multiple vague terms ('competitive', 'probably', 'don't go crazy'), no quantitative thresholds, no defined demand metric, no regulatory consideration, no fairness policy, no quote validity rules, and no stakeholder analysis for couriers. The word 'probably' on guardrails means even the most critical safety feature is uncommitted. This is a vision statement, not a specification. Building from this without clarification would be reckless.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices should correlate positively with demand (BR-1)",
      "System should be automated (BR-3)",
      "Profitability is a goal (BR-7)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail existence and thresholds — 'probably' is not a commitment (BR-4, BR-5)",
      "Definition of 'demand' — is it order volume, capacity utilization, geographic density, time-of-day?",
      "Definition of 'competitive' — no benchmark or measurement (BR-6)",
      "Quote validity — can prices change between quote and checkout?",
      "Courier impact — are driver earnings affected?",
      "Regulatory compliance — price-gouging laws not mentioned",
      "Conflict resolution — when 'competitive' and 'profitable' goals contradict, which wins?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails WILL be implemented despite 'probably' — assuming the CEO intends them but spoke casually",
      "Demand refers to order volume or request rate in a geographic area over a time window",
      "Cost basis for the price floor includes direct delivery costs (fuel, driver, vehicle) but scope of indirect costs is unknown",
      "Real-time means sub-minute price updates, not literally instantaneous",
      "The system serves a courier/delivery business, not a marketplace model"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The term 'demand' is never defined. Is it order volume per hour? Capacity utilization (orders vs available couriers)? Geographic request density? Time-based patterns? The entire pricing model depends on this input and it is completely unspecified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Data Science", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Order volume per time window", "Capacity utilization ratio (orders/couriers)", "Geographic request density", "Composite signal combining multiple factors"],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as capacity utilization ratio (pending orders / available couriers) — this captures both supply and demand sides and directly correlates with delivery cost pressure."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrails are described as 'probably' existing with no thresholds specified. For a fully automated system, undefined guardrails are a critical safety gap. What is the maximum price? What is the minimum? Who sets them? How often can they change?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal", "Finance", "Customers", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["Hard-code initial guardrails (e.g., 0.5x-3x base price)", "Make guardrails configurable per market/region", "Require legal review before setting any guardrail thresholds"],
      "recommendation": "Guardrails must be mandatory (not 'probably'), configurable per market, and reviewed by Legal before launch. Start conservative (e.g., 0.8x-2x base price) and widen based on data."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Competitive' is undefined. Without a benchmark (competitor prices, historical averages, customer willingness-to-pay surveys), this goal is untestable and could conflict directly with the profitability goal.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Finance", "Sales"],
      "options": ["Define competitive as within X% of top 3 competitors", "Define competitive as within X% of own historical average", "Drop 'competitive' as a system constraint and handle via manual market analysis"],
      "recommendation": "Separate concerns: the automated system optimizes for demand-based pricing within guardrails. Competitiveness is handled by periodically adjusting the base price and guardrails based on market analysis — not by the real-time engine."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of courier/driver compensation impact. If delivery price changes dynamically but driver pay is fixed, the company absorbs all variance. If driver pay also fluctuates, this is a fundamentally different system affecting labor relations, driver retention, and potentially labor law compliance.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Couriers/Drivers", "HR/Operations", "Legal"],
      "options": ["Driver pay is fixed — company absorbs price variance", "Driver pay scales with delivery price (e.g., percentage-based)", "Driver pay has its own demand-based model independent of customer pricing"],
      "recommendation": "This must be explicitly decided before implementation. Driver compensation model fundamentally shapes system architecture."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory analysis mentioned. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated or banned in multiple jurisdictions, especially during declared emergencies. A fully automated system operating across regions without jurisdiction-aware guardrails exposes the company to significant legal risk.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal", "Compliance", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Add jurisdiction-aware pricing rules", "Cap all prices at a conservative level that avoids price-gouging triggers", "Launch in unregulated markets only, expand after legal review"],
      "recommendation": "Mandatory legal review per operating jurisdiction before any launch. System must support jurisdiction-specific guardrail overrides."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No quote validity or price-lock policy defined. In real-time pricing, the price can change between when a customer sees it and when they confirm the order. This is a critical UX and trust issue.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Product", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["Lock price for N minutes after quote", "Always charge the price shown at time of order confirmation", "Show price range instead of exact price until confirmation"],
      "recommendation": "Lock the quoted price for a reasonable window (e.g., 5-10 minutes). Charging a different price than shown is a trust-destroying pattern and may violate consumer protection laws."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
